essence: Airlines are a brutal, capital-intensive, perishable-inventory business: an empty seat is lost forever, so load factor + yield management are everything.
Razor-thin and notoriously cyclical margins; fuel and aircraft are huge costs, and pricing is competitive/transparent.
The low-cost-carrier model (high utilisation, single fleet, ancillaries) structurally beats legacy on cost.
High fixed costs + perishable inventory = extreme operating leverage and fragility (small demand/fuel shocks swing P&L wildly).
Travel/OTAs are platform/asset-light; airports are regulated infrastructure - very different economics within the sector.

definition.oneLiner: Companies that move people for travel - airlines, airports, and the online platforms that sell trips.
definition.jobToBeDone: Get people where they want to go - safely, affordably, and on time - and help them find and book the trip.

valueChain.profitPools: Profit pools are thin and volatile in flying itself; the durable pools are ancillaries (bags, seats, fees), loyalty programs (often worth more than the airline), low-cost-carrier cost leadership, airports (regulated infrastructure), and asset-light OTAs (take rate).
valueChain.powerShift: Value has shifted from full-service flying -> low-cost carriers + ancillaries + loyalty -> direct digital distribution and (for travel) online platforms, as cost and distribution control decide survival.

unitEconomics.unitOfRevenue: Revenue per available seat (RASK) vs cost per available seat (CASK); load factor links them
unitEconomics.revenueDrivers: Seats x Load-factor. Revenue = capacity (seats/ASK) x load factor x yield (fare) + ancillaries; the spread between RASK and CASK is the whole game.

costStructure.shape: Huge fixed/semi-fixed costs (aircraft leases, crew, airport fees) + large variable fuel cost; perishable inventory means once a flight departs, empty seats are pure loss - so filling seats (load factor) at a decent yield is existential.
costStructure.leverage: Extreme operating leverage - high load factors are very profitable, but small drops in demand or spikes in fuel turn profit to loss fast; LCCs win by driving down CASK (utilisation, single fleet).

competitionAndMoats.concentration: Consolidating in many markets to a few large carriers; LCCs dominate short-haul; airports are local monopolies (regulated); OTAs concentrated among a few platforms. India: IndiGo dominant, Air India (Tata) #2.

structuralForces.regulation: Heavy - safety, slots, bilateral air-rights, airport charges, and (airports) economic regulation; fuel taxes matter hugely in India.
structuralForces.cyclicality: Highly cyclical & shock-prone - tied to GDP, fuel prices, FX, and exogenous shocks (pandemics, geopolitics).
structuralForces.substitution: High-speed rail (where it exists), video-conferencing (business travel), and for OTAs, direct airline/hotel booking.
structuralForces.supplierBuyerPower: Aircraft duopoly (Airbus/Boeing) and fuel suppliers hold power; airports charge fees; consumers are price-sensitive and (via OTAs) have transparent comparison; GDS/OTAs intermediate distribution.

aiPenetration.maturity: Medium-to-high - revenue management and operations are long-time analytics/AI users; personalisation growing.
aiPenetration.valueChainImpact: AI sharpens the two levers that decide airline P&L - pricing/revenue management (yield) and operations/scheduling (cost, disruption) - and lifts ancillary upsell; for OTAs it powers search/personalisation.
threatsDisruption: AI-native travel platforms / agentic booking disintermediating

ranges: Net margins: thin & volatile (often low single digits)
Ancillary revenue: a large & growing share (LCCs)
Aircraft utilisation: high for LCCs (more block hours)
OTA take rate: varies by segment
benchmarks.note: DIRECTIONAL - rules of thumb, not audited figures. Verify before quoting.

keyTerms.Load factor: % of seats filled - the core utilisation metric.
keyTerms.RASK / CASK: Revenue / Cost per Available Seat Kilometre - the spread is profitability.
keyTerms.Ancillaries: Add-on revenue (bags, seats, food, fees) - a key profit pool.
keyTerms.Slots: Rights to take off/land at congested airports - scarce and valuable.
keyTerms.OTA / GDS: Online Travel Agency / Global Distribution System - booking intermediaries.
keyTerms.Block hours: Hours an aircraft is in use - drives utilisation.

questionsToAskFirst: What's load factor and the RASK-CASK spread?
rookieTraps: Judging at one point in a violently cyclical/shock-prone industry

whatsUniqueHere: Airlines are the textbook example of a great service that's a terrible business: the inventory is perishable (an empty seat at takeoff is gone forever), the costs are huge and fixed, fuel is volatile, and prices are transparently competitive - so the industry famously destroys capital across cycles. The winners survive by obsessive cost leadership (the LCC model) and by monetising what isn't the seat - ancillaries and loyalty programs that are often worth more than the flying itself.

indiaLandscape.marketStructure: A large, fast-growing, price-sensitive aviation market dominated by LCCs: IndiGo is the runaway leader; Air India (Tata) is consolidating Vistara/AirAsia India into the #2 group; Akasa is a new entrant; high fuel taxes and airport costs pressure economics; airports increasingly private (Adani, GMR). Travel/OTAs led by MakeMyTrip.
demandDrivers: Low air-travel penetration + rising middle class
structuralChallenges: Thin, volatile margins & past airline failures
indiaLandscape.note: Semi-perishable - players & policy specifics refresh periodically.

similarTo: Telecom - high fixed cost, price competition, scale

currentState.note: PERISHABLE - refresh via research before quoting. Figures approximate.
currentState.marketStructure: India aviation: IndiGo dominant and expanding (incl. international/widebody); Air India in a multi-year Tata-led turnaround and fleet renewal; record domestic traffic; airport privatisation/expansion continuing; high fuel taxes still a structural drag; AI in pricing and ops scaling.
